text: Sprinters have a more massive body shape with broader shoulders, narrower hips, greater musculature and cellular mass compared to middle and long distance runners.
Long-distance runners are characterized by a slender figure, longer shin segments, and the greatest subcutaneous fat and extracellular mass among running disciplines.
Middle-distance runners are the slimmest among running disciplines, with a narrow trunk and the least subcutaneous fat.
Sprinters and long-distance runners tend toward mesomorphic somatotypes, while middle-distance runners present a more mixed mesomorph-ectomorph body type.

Sprinters have shorter lower legs relative to thigh length, while long-distance runners have proportionally longer shin segments, suggesting lower limb segment ratios differ by running distance specialization.

Overall body size, limb musculature, lower limb length and segment proportions, and body fatness are the principal anthropometric components that distinguish runners across different distance specializations.
